title: Director Nia DaCosta on Making 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple
text: Director Nia DaCosta has been a devoted fan of the franchise since childhood. She watched 28 Days Later regularly at age 12, crediting it with teaching her about directorial vision and authorial voice. When approached to direct the middle installment of the new trilogy, she initially declined, focused on completing her film Hedda. DaCosta emphasized the importance of independence in her filmmaking, stating: I need to be the master of my own destiny. Rather than imitating Danny Boyle's distinctive style, she developed her own visual language for the sequel, creating distinct worlds for different characters that would converge dramatically. The director requested additional infected transformation scenes, including the infamous shirt removal sequence. She carefully balanced the scene's visceral impact with avoiding gratuitous torture imagery, working closely with her editor to modulate intensity while maintaining emotional resonance. DaCosta found unexpected humanity in villain Sir Lord Jimmy Crystal, revealing him as fundamentally lonely beneath his destructive ideology. She highlighted Ralph Fiennes' nuanced performance as Dr. Kelson, hoping for broader industry recognition of his work in horror cinema.
